volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 4.9.993d - Section 5.1.1095)

sections.label: Section 4.9.993d
sections.original: Át enim id quod te iúbeo facias .
sections.translation: Still do you only do that which I bid you .

sections.label: Section 4.9.994
sections.original: Iústumst ut tuós tibi servos tuo árbitratu sérviat .
sections.translation: I ’ ll come . ’ Tis right to do so ; your own servant ought to be obedient at your command .

sections.label: Section 4.9.995
sections.original: Hoc áge sis nunciam .
sections.translation: Give your attention then .

sections.label: Section 4.9.995b
sections.original: Vbi lubet , recita : aúrium operam tíbi dico .
sections.translation: When you choose , read on ; I devote to you the attention of my ears .

sections.label: Section 4.9.996
sections.original: Ceraé quidem haud parsit néque stilo ;
sections.translation: Heyday ! he hasn ’ t been sparing [*] of his wax or pen . But whatever it is , I ’ m resolved to read it through . Attend to me , please , this very instant .

sections.label: Section 4.9.997
sections.original: “ Patér , ducentos Phílippos quaeso Chrýsalo
sections.translation: ( He reads .) Father , I beseech you , do give two hundred Philippeans to Chrysalus , if you wish me to be safe or alive for you —

sections.label: Section 4.9.999
sections.original: malum quidem hercle magnum .
sections.translation: By my troth , really a very great mistake ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.999b
sections.original: Tibi dico .
sections.translation: I tell you .

sections.label: Section 4.9.999c
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.1000
sections.original: Non prius salutem scripsit ?
sections.translation: He hasn ’ t first written the salutation to you .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1000b
sections.original: Nusquam sentio .
sections.translation: I don ’ t see it anywhere .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1001
sections.original: Non dabis , si sapies ; verum si das maxume ,
sections.translation: If you are wise , you ’ ll not give it ; but at the best , if you give it him , why , let him find out some other porter for himself , if he is wise ; for I won ’ t carry it if you order me ever so much . I ’ ve been quite enough suspected as it is , when I was guiltless of a fault .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1005
sections.original: Ausculta porro , dum hoc quod scriptumst perlego .
sections.translation: Listen on now , while I read through what ’ s written .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1006
sections.original: Inde a principio iam inpudens epistula est .
sections.translation: Even from its very commencement the letter is an impertinent one .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1007
sections.original: “ Pudet prodire me ad te in conspectum , pater :
sections.translation: ( Reading .) I am ashamed , father , to appear before you in your presence ; such disgraceful conduct of mine have I heard that you are aware of , that I have formed an intercourse with the wife of a strange Captain .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1010
sections.original: pol haud derides ; nam ducentis aureis
sections.translation: By my troth , you are not joking there ; for I have saved your life from the consequences of this foul deed with two hundred golden Philippeans .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1012
sections.original: Nihil est illorum quin ego illi dixerim .
sections.translation: There ’ s not a particle of these things but what I ’ ve told him .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1013
sections.original: “ Stulte fecisse fateor . sed quaeso , pater ,
sections.translation: I confess that I have acted foolishly . But I beseech you , father , do not forsake me , if , in my folly , I have transgressed .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1015
sections.original: ego animo cupido atque oculis indomitis fui ;
sections.translation: Of fierce desires , and eyes uncontrolled , have I been ; I have been urged on to do a deed of which I am now ashamed . It were proper , then , that you should have taken heed before you were ashamed .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1018
sections.original: Eadem istaec verba dudum illi dixi omnia .
sections.translation: All these very same words did I say to him only just now .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1019
sections.original: “ Quaeso ut sat habeas id , pater , quod Chrysalus
sections.translation: I beseech you , father , to consider that it is enough that Chrysalus

sections.label: Section 4.9.1020
sections.original: me obiurigavit plurumis verbis malis ,
sections.translation: has reproached me with many cutting words , and by his advice has made me a better man , so that ’ tis right that you should return him sincere thanks .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1023
sections.original: Estne istuc istic scriptum ?
sections.translation: Is that written there ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.1023b
sections.original: Em specta , tum scies .
sections.translation: Look and see , then you ’ ll know .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1024
sections.original: Vt qui deliquit supplex est ultro omnibus .
sections.translation: He that has so offended , how humble to all he is , and of his own accord .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1025
sections.original: “ Nunc si me fas est obsecrare abs te , pater ,
sections.translation: Now , father , if even yet I may be allowed to ask anything of you , do give me two hundred Philippean pieces , I do entreat you .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1027
sections.original: Ne unum quidem hercle , si sapis .
sections.translation: By my troth , now , not one even , if you are wise .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1027b
sections.original: Sine perlegam .
sections.translation: Let me read on . ( Reading .) In set form [*] I have taken an oath that I would give this to the woman this day before nightfall ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.1030
sections.original: prius quam á me abiret . nunc , pater , ne perierem
sections.translation: ere she leaves me . Now , father , have a care that I be not forsworn , and take me hence away from her as soon as you can , on whose account I have incurred so much loss and guilt . Take care and let not the two hundred pieces be a cause of annoyance to you ; I will repay you innumerable times as much , if I live .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1035
sections.original: vale atque haec cura ”. quid nunc censes , Chrysale ?
sections.translation: Farewell , and do attend to these matters . Now , Chrysalus , what do you think ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.1036
sections.original: Nihil ego tibi hodie consili quicquam dabo ,
sections.translation: I won ’ t give you one bit of advice this day , and I won ’ t run the risk , that if any mistake is made , you should say that you had done it by my counsel . But , as I conceive , if I were in your place ,

sections.label: Section 4.9.1040
sections.original: dem potius aurum quam illum corrumpi sinam .
sections.translation: I would rather give the gold than suffer him to be corrupted . There is a twofold choice consider which you would adopt ; either to lose the gold , or for a lover to be forsworn . I neither order you , nor forbid you , nor do I persuade you .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1044
sections.original: Miseret me illius .
sections.translation: I ’ m sorry for him .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1044b
sections.original: Tuos est , non mirum facis .
sections.translation: You don ’ t do anything surprising — he is your son .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1045
sections.original: si plus perdundum sit , periisse suaviust ,
sections.translation: If still more must be lost , ’ tis better for it to go , than for this disgrace to be everywhere proclaimed .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1047
sections.original: Ne ille edepol Ephesi multo mavellem foret ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I certainly had much rather he had been at Ephesus , so long as he was well , than that he had returned home . But why don ’ t I hasten to get rid of that which is doomed to be squandered away ?

sections.label: Section 4.9.1050
sections.original: binos ducentos Philippos iam intus ecferam ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll bring you just now from in-doors twice two hundred Philippeans , both those which , a little time since , to my sorrow , I promised to the Captain , and these others . Wait there ; I ’ ll be out to you , Chrysalus , this instant .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1053
sections.original: Fit vasta Troia , scindunt proceres Pergamum .
sections.translation: Troy is laid waste , the chiefs have razed Pergamus . I knew some time ago that I should be the ruin of Pergamus .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1055
sections.original: edepol qui me esse dicat cruciatu malo
sections.translation: I ’ faith I wouldn ’ t dare make a bet with him who should say that I was deserving of severe torture ; so great confusion am I making . But the door makes a noise ; the plunder ’ s being brought forth from Troy . For the present I ’ ll hold my tongue .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1059
sections.original: Cape hoc tibi aurum , Chrysale , i , fer filio .
sections.translation: Take you this gold , Chrysalus ; go , carry it to my son .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1060
sections.original: ego ad forum autem hinc ibo , ut solvam militi .
sections.translation: But I ’ ll go hence to the market-place to pay this to the Captain .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1061
sections.original: Non equidem accipiam . proin tu quaeras qui ferat .
sections.translation: For my part , I ’ ll not receive it ; do you seek somebody at once to take it . I won ’ t have it entrusted to me .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1062b
sections.original: Cape vero , odiose facis .
sections.translation: But do take it ; you ’ re worrying me now .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1063
sections.original: Non equidem capiam .
sections.translation: For my part , I ’ ll not take it .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1063b
sections.original: At quaeso .
sections.translation: But , prithee do .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1063c
sections.original: Dico ut res se habet .
sections.translation: I am telling you what is the fact .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1064
sections.original: Morare .
sections.translation: You are delaying me .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1064b
sections.original: Nolo , inquam , aurum concredi mihi .
sections.translation: I don ’ t want , I say , the gold to be entrusted to me . At all events , find some person to keep a watch upon me .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1065b
sections.original: Ohe , odiose facis .
sections.translation: You ’ re plaguing me .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1066
sections.original: Cedo , sí necesse est .
sections.translation: Well , give it me , if it must be so .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1066b
sections.original: Cura hoc . iam égo huc revenero .—
sections.translation: Take care of it . I ’ ll be back here just now .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1067
sections.original: Curatum est — esse te senem miserrumum .
sections.translation: I ’ ve taken care — that you shall be a most wretched old fellow ; this is bringing an undertaking to a fair ending ; even as it has proved my lot to go rejoicing , laden with the spoil .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1070
sections.original: salute nostra atque urbe capta per dolum
sections.translation: With safety to myself , and the city taken by stratagem , I now bring home my whole army unhurt . But , Spectators , don ’ t you now be surprised that I don ’ t go in triumph ; ’ tis such a common thing , I don ’ t care for it . Still however , the soldiers shall be received with the usual honeyed wine .

sections.label: Section 4.9.1075
sections.original: nunc hanc praedam omnem iam ad quaestorem deferam .—
sections.translation: Now I ’ ll carry off all this booty at once to the Quaestor [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.10.1076
sections.original: Quam mágis in pectore meó foveo quas méus filius turbás turbet ,
sections.translation: The more I revolve it in my breast , what disturbances my son has raised , into what a course of life , and into what habits he unwittingly has headlong plunged himself , the greater is my concern , and the more do I dread lest he may be ruined or corrupted . I know it ; I once was of the same age , and I did all these things ; but in a quiet way .

sections.label: Section 4.10.1080
sections.original: neque plácitant mores quíbus video volgo ín gnatos essé parentes :
sections.translation: I was gay , I had my mistress , I drank , I feasted , I made presents , but still it was seldom I did so . The methods , too , please me not which I see parents in general employ towards their sons . I have determined to give some latitude to my son , that he may have some scope for his inclinations . I think that ’ s right ; but still , I don ’ t wish him to give way too much to sloth and wantonness . Now I ’ m going to Mnesilochus ,

sections.label: Section 4.10.1084a
sections.original: viso écquid eum ad virtútem aut ad
sections.translation: to see whether , as I requested , by his endeavours he has turned him for me to virtue and to sobriety ; as , indeed , I am sure he has done if he has met him , of such a disposition is he by nature .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1087
sections.original: Quicúmque ubi ubi sunt , quí fuerunt quiqué futuri sunt pósthac
sections.translation: Whoever there are in any place whatsoever , whoever have been , and whoever shall be , in time to come , fools , blockheads , idiots , dolts , sots , oafs , lubbers [*] , I singly by far exceed them all in folly and absurd ways .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1090
sections.original: perií , pudet : hocine me aétatis ludós bis factum esse índigne ?
sections.translation: I ’ m undone . I ’ m ashamed of myself ; that I at this time of life should disgracefully have been twice made a fool of ! The more I think of this confusion which my son has made , the more am I incensed . I ’ m ruined , and I ’ m utterly destroyed ; I ’ m distracted in every possible way . All plagues harass me , by all modes of death do I perish . This day has Chrysalus rent me in pieces ; Chrysalus has plundered wretched me ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.1095
sections.original: is mé scelus auro usque áttondit dolis dóctis indoctum , út lubitumst .
sections.translation: he , the villain , by his clever tricks , has shaved , to the very quick , simple me , just as he has pleased . For the Captain says that she is a Courtezan , whom that fellow said was his wife ; and he has informed me of everything , as each particular happened ; how that she had been hired by him for this year ; how that that much gold was left to be repaid [*] , which I , most simple man , had promised him . ’ Tis this , this , I say , through which my breast boils with indignation [*] ; ’ tis this , in fine , by which I am distracted ;
